items.question: Je suis ___. (I am a doctor.)
items.explanation: French uses no article before a job: je suis médecin, not un médecin.

items.question: Marie est ___. (She is a waitress.)
items.explanation: A woman is serveuse; serveur is the masculine form.

items.question: Paul est ___. (He is a teacher.)
items.explanation: For a man use professeur with no article; professeure is the feminine form.

items.question: Tu es ___ ? (Are you a student — male?)
items.explanation: A male student is étudiant, used with no article after es.

items.question: Elle est ___. (She is a nurse.)
items.explanation: The feminine of infirmier is infirmière.

items.question: Il travaille. Il est ___. (He is an engineer.)
items.explanation: No article before a job: il est ingénieur, not un ingénieur.

items.question: Je travaille. Je suis ___. (I am a salesperson — female.)
items.explanation: A woman is vendeuse, and no article follows suis.

items.question: Marie travaille. Elle est ___. (She is a teacher — Canadian female.)
items.explanation: In Canada a female teacher is professeure, and no article is used.

items.question: Paul travaille. Il est ___. (He is a salesperson — male.)
items.explanation: No article and the masculine form: il est vendeur, not un vendeur.
